coredata.dc:title: In-situ embedding of carbon dots in a trisodium citrate crystal matrix for tunable solid-state fluorescence
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Cheng-Long
Zang, Jin-Hao
Lou, Qing
Su, Li-Xia
Li, Zhen
Liu, Zhi-Yu
Dong, Lin
Shan, Chong-Xin
coredata.dc:description: It is still a challenging issue to achieve efficient and tunable fluorescent carbon dot (CD) powders. Herein, we report a novel and general solvothermal strategy to in-situ embedding CDs in trisodium citrate crystal matrix, and these CDs exhibit tunable emission from green to yellow and a high quantum yield up to 21.6% in solid state, comparable to that in solution. This intriguing fluorescence phenomenon may be caused by the space confinement from trisodium citrate crystal matrix, thus enabling CDs to effectively suppress the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching. The CD powders have been employed for fabricating white light-emitting devices on blue chips with adjustable color temperature. Moreover, solid-state lighting systems like flexible fluorescent films and plates have been demonstrated by using the CD powders as the luminescent medium. This work provides a facile way to achieve tunable solid-state fluorescence CD powders, which have promising applications in CDs-based luminescence devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Trisodium citrate
Solid state fluorescence
Composite materials
Phosphors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21 powders

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Nature Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China
Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the support of this research by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51602288 and U1604263 ), the Nature Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China ( 61425021 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2015M582192, 2016T90671), and the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Proposed in situ formation process of fluorescent NCD11 phosphors. The reaction mixture is composed of urea, trisodium citrate and DMF, the formation of NCDs is accompanied with the crystal growth of trisodium citrate, resulting the SSF of NCD11 powders.

(a) TEM image of the NCD11 in trisodium citrate crystal matrix. (b) HRTEM images of the NCD11 in trisodium citrate crystal matrix. (c) TEM image of the NCDs in aqueous solution. (d) HRTEM image of the NCDs in aqueous solution.

(a) XRD pattern of the NCD11 phosphors, trisodium citrate dihydrate treated after 160 °C and trisodium citrate XRD card. (b) FTIR spectra of NCD11 powder and trisodium citrate dihydrate. (c) Full survey of XPS spectrum for the NCD11. (d) High-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s. (e) High-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s. (f) High-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s.

(a) Photograph of the conventional CDs (a1, a4), trisodium citrate dihydrate (a1, a5), and NCD11 powders (a3, a6) under daylight (the upper row) and 365 nm UV lamp illumination (the lower row). (b) UV-vis absorption spectra of the NCD11 powder and NCD11 in aqueous solution. (c) Normalized fluorescence spectra of the NCD11 powder in aqueous solution with different concentration and that of NCDs powder. (d) Time-resolved decay spectra of NCD11 powder and NCD11 in aqueous solution.

(a) Photograph of the NCD11 powder under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp illumination (right). (b) Excitation-emission two-dimensional plot of the NCD11 powder. (c) Photograph of the NCD12 powder under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp illumination (right). (d) Excitation-emission two-dimensional plot of the NCD12 powder. (e) Photograph of the NCD21 powder under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp illumination (right). (f) Excitation-emission two-dimensional plot of the NCD21 powder.

(a-d) The emission spectrum of blue chip (a) and LED based on NCD11 (b), NCD12 (c), and NCD21 (d) powders with the blue chip, respectively. (Inset, photograph of the LEDs operated at 3.2 V and the photograph of LEDs for illumination). (e) The relationship between emission intensity and the operating current of the LEDs. (f) CIE coordinates of NCD11, NCD12, and NCD21 based LED.

(a) Schematic illustration of the preparation process of NCDs/PMMA film (left) and NCDs/epoxy resin plate (right). (b) NCDs/epoxy resin plate under sunlight (top) and 365 nm UV lamp (down) illumination conditions. (c) NCDs/PMMA film under sunlight (top) and 365 nm UV lamp (down) illumination conditions. (d) Schematic of the NCDs/PMMA film for white area light source.
Photophysical characterization of NCD powders under ambient conditions.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: PL quantum yield of CD powder.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: PL quantum yield of CD solution.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The Commission Internationale de L’Éclairage (CIE) under 365 nm UV lamp.

head.ce:title: In-situ embedding of carbon dots in a trisodium citrate crystal matrix for tunable solid-state fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou, Henan, 450000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is still a challenging issue to achieve efficient and tunable fluorescent carbon dot (CD) powders. Herein, we report a novel and general solvothermal strategy to in-situ embedding CDs in trisodium citrate crystal matrix, and these CDs exhibit tunable emission from green to yellow and a high quantum yield up to 21.6% in solid state, comparable to that in solution. This intriguing fluorescence phenomenon may be caused by the space confinement from trisodium citrate crystal matrix, thus enabling CDs to effectively suppress the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching. The CD powders have been employed for fabricating white light-emitting devices on blue chips with adjustable color temperature. Moreover, solid-state lighting systems like flexible fluorescent films and plates have been demonstrated by using the CD powders as the luminescent medium. This work provides a facile way to achieve tunable solid-state fluorescence CD powders, which have promising applications in CDs-based luminescence devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Solid-state lighting devices, especially white-light-emitting diodes (WLEDs), as the next-generation lighting sources and displaying devices have recently received extensive attention owing to their long service life, excellent energy saving, and good color rendition [1–8]. Nowadays, WLEDs are always manufactured by combining UV chips with mixture of blue, green and red phosphors or utilizing blue chips with the mixture of yellow phosphors Currently, the conventional WLED phosphors are always limited in rare-earth materials or Cd2+ based semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [7,9,10]. However, the rare-earth elements are very limited in reserve and complicated for recycling. Moreover, the existing of Cd in QDs may cause serious environmental concerns due to its toxicity. Therefore, it is of high significance to develop low-cost and eco-friendly WLED phosphors.

Luminescent carbon dots (CDs) as emerging carbon-based nanomaterials have aroused increasing attraction in various fields, such as bioimaging, sensors, photocatalysis and light-emitting diodes due to their stability, low toxicity, low cost and high quantum yield, and CDs have been considered a promising candidate that may be used in flat panel displays and lighting sources [5,11–23]. However, aggregation-induced luminescence quenching is still one of the issues have to be solved urgently for such applications of CDs [24–30]. And efficient solid-state fluorescence (SSF) are difficult to obtain for CD powders owing to the overmuch resonance energy transfer (RET) or direct π-π interactions when CDs are jointed with each other in solid state [28]. These drawbacks not only limit the application of CDs as phosphors in WLEDs but also the application fields such as electroluminescent devices, luminescent solar concentrators, and so on [4,31–33]. Nowadays, the usual approach of conquering the luminescence quenching behavior is mostly incorporating CDs solution with high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) into solid-state matrices, such as inorganic salts, silica, polymers, layered and mesoporous materials [34–37]. Nevertheless, these methods are always complex and difficult to retain the outstanding fluorescent properties of CD aqueous solution [19,38,39]. Although solid-state luminescent CDs using poly (vinyl alcohol) as matrix achieved high PLQYs of 84%, the loading fraction of CDs is quite low, only 0.6 wt% [38,39]. Besides, high loading fraction still results in the severe decrease in the PLQYs of CDs mainly due to the reuniting in the solid state during the preparation process. Moreover, for WLEDs, high color rendering index and low light toxicity can be guaranteed by the tunable emission of the CD phosphors. Hence, it is of great significance to develop multiple color emissive and self-quenching-resistant CDs for practical applications.

In this work, a facile and general strategy has been developed to synthesized novel CDs with tunable solid-state fluorescence by embedding CDs within trisodium citrate crystalline matrices in solvothermal conditions. The CD powders are obtained with tunable emission color from green to yellow through adjusting the reaction solvents. These relative PLQYs of the CD powders with green, yellow-green, and yellow emission reach up to 17.6%, 18.7%, and 21.6%, which are comparable to those in solution. Employing the multicolor emissive CD powders as phosphors, WLEDs with tunable color temperatures using a 450 nm blue chip as the excitation source have been fabricated. Furthermore, different kinds of CD-based optical devices like flexible fluorescent films and flat panel have been achieved by using polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) or epoxy resin as a solid matrix to expanding the application of CDs. Achieving tunable solid-state fluorescence of CDs with one-step solvothermal process may largely simplify the preparation of WLEDs and meet the requirements of various optoelectronic devices.
The chemicals reagents were analytical grade and used as received without further purification. All of the chemicals purchased from Aladdin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21 powders
ce:section.ce:para: 1.5 g trisodium citrate dihydrate and 2 g urea were dissolved in 10 ml dimethylformamide (DMF), dimethylacetamide (DMAC), and diethylformamide (DEF), respectively. Then the solution were transferred to an autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. After that, the reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained products dissolved in methanol in order to obtain the NCD powders and these corresponding obtained samples named NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21 powders, respectively.

The structural properties of the NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21 powders were characterized using a JEM-2010 transmission electron microscope and Bruker D8 Discover (Germany) x-ray diffractometer. The powders were measured using Thermo ESCALAB-250 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) was measured by Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments). The fluorescence properties of the NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21 powders were collected in a Hitachi F-7000 spectrophotometer. The absorption spectra of the samples were acquired in a Shimadzu UV-3101PC spectrometer. The fluorescence decay curves of the CD powders were measured by Horiba FL-322 using a 405 nm NanoLED monitoring the emission of NCD11, NCD12, and NCD21 powders and solution at 537, 530, and 513 nm, respectively. The PLQYs of NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21 powders were measured in a calibrated integrating sphere in FLS920 spectrometer. The current-voltage (I-V) characteristics of the commercial Si solar cell, laterally coupled to the LSC, were measured by a Keysight 2900A SourceMeter under simulated sunlight. The performance of CD based WLEDs were characterized by a Photo Research PR-655 spectroradiometer.

1 g citric acid and 2 g urea were dissolved in 10 ml DMF/DMAC/DEF. Then the solution was transferred to an autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. After that, the reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally. The obtained products dissolved in ethanol were centrifuged (8000 rpm, 20 min) and these corresponding obtained samples named CD11, CD12 and CD21 powders, respectively.

InGaN blue LED chips with central emission of 450 nm were purchased Shenzhen Mingto photoelectric technology co. Ltd. The operating voltage of the blue LED was 3.0 V. For preparation WLED, 1 g epoxy resin glue A and 3 g glue B was mixed, then NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21 phosphors were mixed with the epoxy resin glue. After coating the mixture onto the blue LED and dried at atmosphere, the WLED can be obtained.

For the preparation of NCDs-based fluorescent plates, 3 g epoxy resin glue A and 9 g glue B was mixed, then 1 g NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21 powders were dissolved in 1 mL methanol. Mixing the epoxy resin glue and CD and stirring for 24 h, the raw products were dried at atmosphere. Then the fluorescent plates based on CD powders can be obtained.

For the preparation of NCDs-based films, the NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21 powders were mixed with the PMMA with mass ratios of 3:1 in DMF. After drying the mixture at atmosphere, the films based on NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21 powders can be obtained. When coating the film on 450 nm blue LED, a white area light source was obtained.

In this work, a facile and general route to multiple color emissive and self-quenching-resistant CD powders has been developed through an in situ solvothermal process. As shown in Fig. 1 , the reaction mixture is composed of trisodium citrate (carbon sources), urea (nitrogen sources) and organic solvents. First, the CDs are formed through the chemical reaction of the carbon sources and nitrogen sources under the solvothermal condition. With the development of the reaction, excess trisodium citrate begins to crystallize around the resultant CDs at high temperature. Then, the CDs can be in situ embedded and constrained in the trisodium citrate matrix during the crystal growth. At last, the CD powders with solid-state fluorescence are precipitated and obtained from the reaction solution. The emission colors of the CD powders are regulated by changing the reaction solvents with DMF, DMAC, and DEF, and these corresponding obtained samples named NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21 powders, respectively.

Taking NCD11 as an example, the morphology had been characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 (a) and (b), the as-synthesized NCD11 powders are constituted by sphere-like submicron crystal morphology with an average particle diameter of around 400 nm, which well matches with that measured by the dynamic light scattering (DLS, Fig. S3a). The corresponding high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image demonstrates the crystal particles exhibit crystal well-resolved lattice fringes with a lattice space of 0.236 nm, which is corresponding to the (100) plane of trisodium citrate crystal matrix as shown in Fig. 2(b). Moreover, the embedded NCD11 shows a uniform distribution with an average particle diameter of 4.5 nm as shown in Fig. 2(c). Meanwhile, the HRTEM image reveals a well-resolved lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which fits well with the (100) facet of graphitic graphite as shown in Fig. 2(d). In addition, the other CD powders (NCD12 and NCD21) perform the same morphology with the NCD11 (Figs. S2–5, Supporting Information).

The crystalline structures of the CD powders have been confirmed by the powder x-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. As the XRD revealed in Fig. 3 (a), the NCD11 powders exhibit the characteristic diffraction peak of the trisodium citrate dihydrate structure. In addition, the trisodium citrate dihydrate powders treated under the same hydrothermal conditions at 160 °C for 4 h perform a similar crystalline structure with NCD11 powders, but no CDs and relevant photoluminescent phenomenon are observed in the mother liquid. The surface functional groups and elemental characteristics of the CD powders have been investigated through various spectroscopy characterizations. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the NCD11 powders and trisodium citrate dihydrate are shown in Fig. 3(b). The peaks at 3478 cm−1 and 3273 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H and C-H, the peaks at 1662 cm−1 and 1588 cm−1 are contributed from sp2 C-H (C=C/C=N), and the peak at 1395 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of C=O, respectively. Notably, new characteristic band emerging at 619 cm−1 in the NCD11 powders similar to that in the trisodium citrate dihydrate crystals are ascribed to the wagging vibrations of O-Na, which further indicates CDs can be embedded in the trisodium citrate dihydrate crystalline matrix. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) has been employed to characterize the surface groups of the CD powders. As shown in the full survey of XPS spectra in Fig. 3(c), there are four kinds of elements (C, N, O, and Na) existed in the NCD11 powders. The high resolution C1s XPS envelope can be deconvoluted into three Gaussian peaks at 284.6, 286.1, 287.9 and 289.0 eV in Fig. 3(d), which are attributed to sp2 C (C-C/C=C), C-N, C=O, and C(O)-O groups, respectively. Moreover, for N1s, XPS spectra can be fitted into two kinds of N species, pyrrolic N at 397.8 eV and graphitic N at 399.5 eV in Fig. 3(e). The O XPS spectrum of the NCD11 displays two main peaks at 530.8 and 531.7 eV as shown in Fig. 3(f), corresponding to quinone O and carbonyl O (C=O), respectively. Significantly, the CD powders from other solvothermal synthesis (DMAC or DEF) demonstrate the similar crystalline structures and surface groups (Figs. S6–9, Supporting Information).

With the strategy of the NCD11 powders, the NCD12 and NCD21 powders synthesized by modulating the reaction solvents with DMAC and DEF also exhibit the solid-state fluorescence with high PLQYs. The photograph of NCD11, NCD12, and NCD21 powders under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp is shown in Fig. 5 (a), 5(c), and 5(e). The three CD powders reveal a little color difference under sunlight and emit strong fluorescence from yellow to green. As shown in Fig. 5(b), 5(d), and 5(f), the as-prepared NCD11, NCD12, and NCD21 powders show the characteristic excitation-dependent fluorescence property of CDs with different excitation-emission centers at (422 nm, 537 nm), (412 nm, 530 nm) and (414 nm, 513 nm), respectively. Correspondingly, the observed emissions of the CD powders can gradually shift from yellow to green with changing the reaction solvents [40]. Therefore, color adjustable of CD powders with solid-state fluorescence could be achieved by only changing the appropriate solvents in solvothermal conditions. The PLQYs of NCD12 and NCD21 powders measured up to 18.7%, 17.6% are still comparable to those of NCD12 and NCD21 aqueous solution (14.9% and 17.5%) under the same excitation. Besides, the NCD12 and NCD21 powders present longer fluorescence lifetimes (5.48 ns and 13.56 ns) than those in aqueous solution (4.2 ns and 13.5 ns) with the same reason as NCD11 (Fig. S11, Supporting Information). All the CD powders reveal a little color difference under sunlight and emit strong fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp with the Commission Internationale de L’Éclairage (CIE) coordinates of (0.30, 0.55), (0.27, 0.55) and (0.21, 0.54) for NCD11, NCD12, and NCD21 powders, respectively. And the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching still resides in CDs with the two other reaction solvents (DMAC and DEF), if the trisodium citrate matrix is replaced by citric acid in the synthesis condition (Fig. S12, Supporting Information).

On the basis of the above observation, these emission tunable CD powders can be successfully prepared by in situ embedding CDs into trisodium citrate matrix in the presence of different organic solvents. The emission wavelength of ideal CDs are depending on the size of sp2 domains [41–43]. From the XPS spectra of the C1s (Fig. S13), it is found that the sp2 C (C-C/C=C) at 284.6 eV decreases from 50.9% to 47.3% for NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21, which indicate that the graphitization degree of the CD samples will decrease by changing the reaction solvents with DMF, DMAC, and DEF, respectively. According to Sun and co-workers’ report, the high extent of graphitization in CDs stands for the extended effective π-conjugation length or large sp2 domain [43]. Conversely, with the decrease of graphitization degree, the emission band of CDs can significantly blue shift from yellow to green with DMF, DMAC, and DEF as reaction solvents. The above mentioned study shows that the as-prepared CD powders have tunable solid-state fluorescence, which makes them possible for the efficient phosphors in WLEDs.

The CD based phosphors exhibit from yellow to green fluorescence emission under 365 nm UV lamp and show an excitation-dependent fluorescence under the variety of excitation wavelengths. With the increase of excitation wavelength, the emission of three kinds of the CD phosphors shows a similar red shift. Therefore, the WLED may be achieved solely by those CD phosphors with appropriate commercial blue chips. In this work, 450 nm emissive InGaN chips are chosen for fabricating WLEDs through covering CD phosphors with epoxy resin, which is always used in LED packing. As shown in Fig. 6 (a)–(d), the UV chip, NCD11, NCD12 and NCD21 based LEDs exhibit blue, white, warm white and blue color with the CIE coordinates of (0.16, 0.08), (0.35, 0.42), (0.38, 0.49) and (0.17, 0.19), respectively ( Fig. 6f). Therein, the WLED can be achieved in NCD11 and NCD12 based LEDs approaching to the coordinate (0.33, 0.33) of pure white light. In addition, the color temperature (CT) regulation can be achieved in the NCD11 and NCD12 based LEDs from 5054 K to 4492 K. Besides, the three CD phosphors show quite good photostability, where the emission intensity can be kept near 100% under 365 nm UV irradiation for about 2 h (Fig. S13, Supporting Information). Furthermore, a nearly linear relation exists between the emission intensity of the three CD based LEDs and the applied current as shown in Fig. 6(e). And emission spectra of the three CD based LEDs almost keep the same under different applied current, even under a high current of 100 mA (Figs. S17–19, Supporting Information). And the thermostability of NCD based LEDs is shown in Fig. S16. When the temperature increases from 20 to 160 °C, the luminous intensity of the NCD11, NCD12, and NCD21 based LEDs is about 50, 20, and 65% lower at 160 °C than at 20 °C owing to high-temperature induced PL quenching [44].

As shown in Fig. S20, the efficacy of the three CDs based LEDs has a little decline with the increase in the applied power of the LEDs. When the applied power of the LEDs is 0.125 W, the efficacy of the LEDs with NCD11, NCD12, and NCD21 as phosphors can reach about 15, 22, and 63 lm W−1, which is comparable to the value of reported CD based LEDs (Table S1).

Furthermore, the NCD phosphors also could be used in different forms of luminescent devices to meet the requirement of various applications. As shown in Fig. 7 (a), the fabrication of a white-light flexible luminescent films or plastic plates is based on the CD powders as the luminescent mediums. In this case, poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) and epoxy resin are used as matrix. The NCD11/epoxy resin plate is nearly transparent under sunlight and emits green color light concentrated along the edge of plates under 365 nm UV lamp as shown in Fig. 7(b). As shown in Fig. S21, we can find that the luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) based on the NCDs show a high increase of conversion efficiency for solar energy in comparison to the blank one. And the conversion efficiency using NCD11, NCD12, and NCD21 as LSCs increases by about 46%, 43%, and 4%, respectively, illustrating that the CDs are suitable to be used as luminescent solar concentrators (LSC). The flexible CD powders/PMMA films are a little yellow under sunlight and emit different color fluorescence under 360 nm UV lamp as shown in Fig. 7(c). The NCD11/PMMA film could emit bright white fluorescence as an area light source when it is covered on the top of a blue chip as shown in Fig. 7(d). This indicates that these CD powders can be used as a versatile optical material serving for different fields of application.

In summary, one facile strategy has been developed to synthesize tailoring emission CD powders with high efficient solid-state fluorescence. The luminescent CDs are in-situ confined in the trisodium citrate crystal matrix uniformly, which can effectively prevent the CDs from aggregation-induced luminescence quenching in the solid state. Besides, the CDs powders have the solid-state fluorescence property and can be employed for fabricating WLEDs on blue chips with tunable color temperature. Furthermore, solid-state lighting devices like flexible fluorescent films and fluorescent plates have been demonstrated by using the CD powders as the luminescent medium. This work provides a facile way to achieve tunable solid-state fluorescence CD powders, which have promising applications in CDs-based luminescence devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support of this research by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51602288 and U1604263), the Nature Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars of China (61425021), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2015M582192, 2016T90671), and the Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province.

Carbon nanodots as dual-mode nanosensors for selective detection of hydrogen peroxide

Solid-state carbon dots with red fluorescence and effcient construction of dual-fluorescence morphologies
A self-quenching-resistant carbon-dot powder with tunable solid-state fluorescence and construction of dual-fluorescence morphologies for white light-emission
Long lifetime photoluminescence in N, S co-doped carbon quantum dots from an ionic liquid and their applications in ultrasensitive detection of pesticides
One-step microwave-assisted polyol synthesis of green luminescent carbon dots as optical nanoprobes
Water-triggered luminescent "nano-bombs" based on supra-(carbon nanodots)

Colloidal carbon dots based highly stable luminescent solar concentrators
Preparation and application of carbon-nanodot@NaCl composite phosphors with strong green emission

Yellow-emitting carbon nanodots and their flexible and transparent films for white LEDs
Layer-by-layer assembly of carbon dots-based ultrathin films with enhanced quantum yield and temperature sensing performance
Highly luminescent carbon-nanoparticle-based materials: factors influencing photoluminescence quantum yield
Conquering aggregation-induced solid-state luminescence quenching of carbon dots through a carbon dots-triggered silica gelation process
Full-color inorganic carbon dot phosphors for white-light-emitting diodes
Tailoring color emissions from N-doped graphene quantum dots for bioimaging applications
Graphitic nitrogen triggers red fluorescence in carbon dots
Synthesis of carbon dots with multiple color emission by controlled graphitization and surface functionalization
Electrostatic assembly guided synthesis of highly luminescent carbon-nanodots@BaSO4 hybrid phosphors with improved stability
